news_summary: Advancements in AI, innovative therapies, and deeper understanding of stress-related conditions are driving significant progress in cancer prediction, cholesterol reduction, and eczema management.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière d'intelligence artificielle, les thérapies innovantes et une meilleure compréhension des troubles liés au stress permettent de réaliser des progrès significatifs dans le domaine du dépistage du cancer, de la réduction du cholestérol et de la prise en charge de l'eczéma.
news_summary_es: Los avances en inteligencia artificial, las terapias innovadoras y una mayor comprensión de los trastornos relacionados con el estrés están impulsando avances significativos en la predicción del cáncer, la reducción del colesterol y el tratamiento del eccema.

articles.title: New AI tool predicts cancer spread with surprising accuracy
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered that cancer spread isn’t random—it follows a kind of biological “program.” By studying colon tumor cells, they identified gene patterns that signal whether a cancer is likely to metastasize. Their AI model, MangroveGS, can predict this risk with about 80% accuracy and even works across multiple cancer types. This could transform how doctors decide who needs aggressive treatment and who doesn’t.
articles.content:
Why do some tumors spread while others remain localized? Scientists still do not fully understand what controls a cancer cell's ability to metastasize, but answering this question is essential for improving patient care. Researchers at the University of Geneva (UNIGE) studied cells from colon cancer and identified key factors that influence whether a tumor is likely to spread. They also uncovered specific gene expression patterns that can be used to estimate that risk.

Building on these findings, the team developed an artificial intelligence tool (MangroveGS) that converts these genetic signals into highly reliable predictions across multiple cancer types. The study, published in Cell Reports, could lead to more personalized treatments and help uncover new therapeutic targets.

Cancer as a Distorted Development Process

"The origin of cancer is often attributed to 'anarchic cells'," explains Ariel Ruiz i Altaba, professor in the Department of Genetic Medicine and Development at the UNIGE Faculty of Medicine, who led the study. "However, cancer should rather be understood as a distorted form of development." Genetic and epigenetic changes can reactivate biological programs that are normally turned off after early development, ultimately driving tumor formation.

Rather than being random, cancer appears to follow structured biological rules. "The challenge is therefore to find the keys to understanding its logic and form. And, in the case of metastases, to identify the characteristics of the cells that will separate from the tumor to create another one elsewhere in the body."

Tracking Metastatic Cancer Cells

Metastasis is responsible for most cancer deaths, especially in colon, breast, and lung cancers. By the time cancer cells are detected circulating in the blood or lymphatic system, the disease has often already begun to spread. Although scientists understand many of the mutations that lead to tumor formation, no single genetic change explains why some cells break away and migrate while others remain in place.

"The difficulty lies in being able to determine the complete molecular identity of a cell - an analysis that destroys it - while observing its function, which requires it to remain alive," explains Professor Ruiz i Altaba. To overcome this, the researchers isolated, cloned, and grew tumor cells in the lab. "These clones were then evaluated in vitro and in a mouse model to observe their ability to migrate through a real biological filter and generate metastases," adds Arwen Conod.

Gene Signatures Linked to Cancer Spread

The team analyzed the activity of hundreds of genes in about thirty cell clones taken from two primary colon tumors. This revealed clear gene expression patterns that closely matched each cell's ability to move and spread. Importantly, metastatic potential was not determined by a single cell's profile, but by how groups of related cancer cells interact with each other.

AI Tool Predicts Metastasis Risk

The researchers integrated these gene signatures into an artificial intelligence system. "The great novelty of our tool, called 'Mangrove Gene Signatures (MangroveGS)', is that it exploits dozens, even hundreds, of gene signatures. This makes it particularly resistant to individual variations," explains Aravind Srinivasan.

After training, the model was able to predict metastasis and colon cancer recurrence with nearly 80% accuracy, outperforming existing methods. The same gene signatures derived from colon cancer also proved useful in predicting metastatic risk in other cancers, including stomach, lung, and breast cancer.

Toward More Personalized Cancer Care

MangroveGS can work directly with tumor samples collected in hospitals. Cells are analyzed, their RNA is sequenced, and a metastasis risk score is quickly generated and shared securely with doctors and patients through an encrypted platform.

"This information will prevent the overtreatment of low-risk patients, thereby limiting side effects and unnecessary costs, while intensifying the monitoring and treatment of those at high risk," says Ariel Ruiz i Altaba. "It also offers the possibility of optimizing the selection of participants in clinical trials, reducing the number of volunteers required, increasing the statistical power of studies, and providing therapeutic benefits to the patients who need it most."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in cancer research where an AI tool predicts metastasis risk with high accuracy across multiple cancer types, enabling more personalized and effective treatment decisions. This has broad implications for improving patient outcomes and reducing overtreatment, representing a meaningful positive impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New AI tool predicts cancer spread with high accuracy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer spreads when cells move from the original tumor to other parts of the body, a process called metastasis. This spreading is the main cause of death in many cancers, but scientists have not fully understood why some tumors spread while others don’t.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Geneva studied colon cancer cells and found patterns in gene activity that show which tumors are likely to spread. They created an artificial intelligence (AI) tool named MangroveGS that uses these gene patterns to predict the risk of metastasis. The AI was about 80% accurate and worked for several types of cancer, like colon, lung, breast, and stomach cancers.

**Impact** – This tool helps doctors know which patients need stronger treatment and which don’t, reducing unnecessary side effects and costs. It also improves how clinical trials are done by selecting patients more carefully. Unlike older methods, MangroveGS looks at many genes together, making its predictions more reliable.

**What's next step** – Hospitals can start using MangroveGS to test tumor samples and give doctors quick risk scores. Researchers will likely explore new treatments targeting the gene patterns found, and the AI could be tested with more cancer types to help even more patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new AI tool uses gene patterns to accurately predict cancer spread, improving treatment choices and patient care across several cancer types.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel outil d'IA prédit avec précision la propagation du cancer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer se propage lorsque des cellules quittent la tumeur d'origine pour aller dans d'autres parties du corps, un processus appelé métastase. Cette propagation est la principale cause de décès dans de nombreux cancers, mais les scientifiques ne comprennent pas encore complètement pourquoi certaines tumeurs se propagent et d'autres non.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Genève ont étudié des cellules du cancer du côlon et identifié des motifs dans l'activité des gènes qui montrent quels cancers risquent de se propager. Ils ont créé un outil d'intelligence artificielle (IA) nommé MangroveGS qui utilise ces motifs pour prédire le risque de métastase. L'IA a atteint environ 80 % de précision et fonctionne pour plusieurs types de cancers, comme le côlon, le poumon, le sein et l'estomac.

**Impact** – Cet outil aide les médecins à savoir quels patients ont besoin d'un traitement plus fort et lesquels non, réduisant ainsi les effets secondaires inutiles et les coûts. Il améliore aussi la sélection des patients pour les essais cliniques. Contrairement aux méthodes précédentes, MangroveGS analyse plusieurs gènes en même temps, ce qui rend ses prédictions plus fiables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les hôpitaux peuvent commencer à utiliser MangroveGS pour tester les échantillons de tumeurs et fournir rapidement un score de risque aux médecins. Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer de nouveaux traitements ciblant les motifs génétiques découverts, et l'IA pourrait être testée sur d'autres types de cancers pour aider encore plus de patients.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouvel outil d'IA utilise des motifs génétiques pour prédire avec précision la propagation du cancer, améliorant les choix de traitement et les soins pour plusieurs types de cancers.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva herramienta de IA predice con alta precisión la propagación del cáncer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer se propaga cuando las células salen del tumor original y viajan a otras partes del cuerpo, un proceso llamado metástasis. Esta propagación es la principal causa de muerte en muchos tipos de cáncer, pero los científicos no entienden completamente por qué algunos tumores se diseminan y otros no.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Ginebra estudiaron células de cáncer de colon y encontraron patrones en la actividad de los genes que indican qué tumores probablemente se diseminarán. Crearon una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (IA) llamada MangroveGS que usa estos patrones para predecir el riesgo de metástasis. La IA tuvo cerca del 80% de precisión y funcionó para varios tipos de cáncer, como colon, pulmón, mama y estómago.

**Impacto** – Esta herramienta ayuda a los médicos a decidir quién necesita tratamientos más fuertes y quién no, reduciendo efectos secundarios y costos innecesarios. También mejora la selección de pacientes para ensayos clínicos. A diferencia de métodos anteriores, MangroveGS analiza muchos genes juntos, haciendo las predicciones más confiables.

**Próximo paso** – Los hospitales podrán usar MangroveGS para analizar muestras de tumores y dar a los médicos puntajes rápidos de riesgo. Los investigadores probablemente buscarán nuevos tratamientos basados en los patrones genéticos descubiertos, y la IA podría probarse en más tipos de cáncer para ayudar a más pacientes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva herramienta de IA usa patrones genéticos para predecir con precisión la propagación del cáncer, mejorando las decisiones de tratamiento y la atención en varios tipos de cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a network of glowing, interconnected cell-like structures with delicate, branching pathways symbolizing gene expression patterns, flowing into a stylized, protective barrier that filters and guides some cells away like gentle currents, all rendered in soft natural tones of greens, blues, and warm earth colors to evoke hope and scientific breakthrough.

articles.title: New pill cuts “bad” cholesterol by 60% in major trial
articles.summary: A new pill, enlicitide, reduced LDL (“bad”) cholesterol by about 60% in a large clinical trial, matching the power of injectable therapies. Because it’s taken orally, it could overcome one of the biggest barriers keeping patients from using current treatments. Researchers say many people still don’t reach safe cholesterol levels—even on statins—highlighting the need for better options.
articles.content:
A new experimental pill called enlicitide dramatically lowered levels of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, often referred to as "bad" cholesterol, by as much as 60%, according to a phase three clinical trial published in The New England Journal of Medicine. If the drug receives approval from the Food and Drug Administration, it could offer millions of people in the United States a new way to reduce their risk of heart attacks and strokes.

"Fewer than half of patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease currently reach LDL cholesterol goals. An oral therapy this effective has the potential to dramatically improve our ability to prevent heart attacks and strokes on a population level," said Ann Marie Navar, M.D., Ph.D., a cardiologist and Associate Professor of Internal Medicine and in the Peter O'Donnell Jr. School of Public Health at UT Southwestern Medical Center. Dr. Navar led the study, which was sponsored by the drugmaker Merck & Co. Inc.

Why Lowering LDL Cholesterol Matters

For decades, scientists have understood that LDL cholesterol plays a central role in cardiovascular disease. These cholesterol particles can build up inside artery walls in a process known as atherosclerosis. Over time, this buildup can block blood flow and lead to heart attacks or strokes. Because of this, reducing LDL cholesterol is a key strategy both for preventing heart disease and for lowering risk in people who already have it.

From Nobel Prize Discovery to New Treatments

According to Dr. Navar, enlicitide builds on decades of scientific work at UT Southwestern. Years ago, researchers Michael Brown, M.D., and Joseph Goldstein, M.D., identified the LDL receptor on liver cells, which helps remove LDL cholesterol from the bloodstream. Their discovery earned the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1985 and paved the way for statins, the most widely used cholesterol-lowering drugs today.

Later, findings from the Dallas Heart Study at UTSW, led by Helen Hobbs, M.D., and Jonathan Cohen, Ph.D., revealed that some people naturally have lower LDL cholesterol due to genetic changes that reduce production of the PCSK9 protein. This protein limits the number of LDL receptors on liver cells, making it harder for the body to clear cholesterol. That insight led to the development of injectable PCSK9 inhibitors, including monoclonal antibodies and RNA-based therapies. Drugs such as evolocumab and alirocumab can lower LDL cholesterol by about 60%.

Why Existing Treatments Are Underused

Even though these injectable treatments are highly effective, they are not widely used in everyday care. Dr. Navar noted that earlier challenges included high costs and insurance barriers. While those issues have improved, many physicians still hesitate to prescribe them. One likely reason is that these medications must be given as injections rather than taken as pills.

How Enlicitide Works

Enlicitide targets the same PCSK9 pathway as those injectable drugs, attaching to the protein in the bloodstream to help the body remove LDL cholesterol more efficiently. The key difference is that enlicitide is taken orally once a day, making it a simpler option for patients.

Clinical Trial Results Show 60% LDL Reduction

The phase three trial included 2,909 participants who either had atherosclerosis or were considered at risk due to related health conditions. About two-thirds received enlicitide, while the rest were given a placebo. Most participants were already taking statins, yet their average LDL cholesterol level remained 96 milligrams per deciliter (mg/dl), well above recommended targets of 70 mg/dl for those with atherosclerosis and 55 mg/dl for those at risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.

"The study population reflects what we see in clinical practice," Dr. Navar said. "Even the highest intensity statins are often not enough to get people to their cholesterol goals."

After 24 weeks, patients taking enlicitide saw their LDL cholesterol drop by about 60% compared with those on a placebo. The drug also lowered other important markers linked to cardiovascular disease, including non-HDL lipoprotein cholesterol, apolipoprotein B, and lipoprotein(a). These improvements were maintained over a full year of follow-up.

"These reductions in LDL cholesterol are the most we have ever achieved with an oral drug by far since the development of statins," Dr. Navar said.

What Comes Next

Another clinical trial is already underway to determine whether these cholesterol reductions will translate into fewer heart attacks and strokes.

Dr. Brown, a Regental Professor, holds the Paul J. Thomas Chair in Medicine and the W.A. (Monty) Moncrief Distinguished Chair in Cholesterol and Arteriosclerosis Research. Dr. Goldstein, a Regental Professor, holds the Julie and Louis A. Beecherl, Jr. Distinguished Chair in Biomedical Research and the Paul J. Thomas Chair in Medicine. Dr. Hobbs holds the Dallas Heart Ball Chair in Cardiology Research and is a member of the Harold C. Simmons Comprehensive Cancer Center. Dr. Cohen holds the C. Vincent Prothro Distinguished Chair in Human Nutrition Research.

This study was funded by Merck Sharp & Dohme, a subsidiary of Merck.

Dr. Navar received consulting fees from Merck for part of the work on this study. She also received fees for other consulting work from Merck and from other pharmaceutical companies that make lipid-lowering drugs (as disclosed in the study).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major breakthrough in cardiovascular health with a new oral pill, enlicitide, that reduces LDL cholesterol by 60%, matching injectable therapies but with easier administration. This has broad significance as it could improve prevention of heart attacks and strokes for millions who struggle to reach cholesterol goals with current treatments. The article provides detailed clinical trial results and context on the scientific background, demonstrating a substantial positive real-world impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New pill cuts bad cholesterol by 60%, matching powerful injections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people have high levels of LDL cholesterol, known as "bad" cholesterol, which can block blood flow and cause heart attacks or strokes. Current treatments include pills called statins and injections that lower LDL, but injections can be hard for patients to use.

**What happened** – A new pill called enlicitide was tested in a large clinical trial with nearly 3,000 participants. Taken once a day, it lowered LDL cholesterol by about 60%, the same amount as strong injectable drugs. Most participants were already on statins but still had high cholesterol. The trial lasted 24 weeks, and the results stayed strong after a year.

**Impact** – This is the first oral drug to reduce LDL cholesterol this much, making it easier for many people to take. Since injections can be expensive or inconvenient, an effective pill could help more patients reach safer cholesterol levels and reduce their risk of heart disease.

**What's next step** – Researchers are now running another trial to see if enlicitide actually lowers the number of heart attacks and strokes. If approved by health authorities, it could become a new, simpler treatment option.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The new pill enlicitide offers a powerful, easy-to-take option to lower bad cholesterol by 60%, potentially helping millions prevent heart problems.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle pilule réduit le mauvais cholestérol de 60 %, à l’égal des injections puissantes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes ont un taux élevé de cholestérol LDL, appelé « mauvais » cholestérol, qui peut bloquer le flux sanguin et causer des crises cardiaques ou des AVC. Les traitements actuels comprennent des pilules appelées statines et des injections, mais ces dernières sont parfois difficiles à utiliser.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle pilule nommée enlicitide a été testée dans un grand essai clinique avec près de 3 000 participants. Prise une fois par jour, elle a réduit le cholestérol LDL d’environ 60 %, autant que les médicaments injectables puissants. La plupart des participants prenaient déjà des statines mais avaient encore un taux élevé. L’essai a duré 24 semaines, et les résultats sont restés stables après un an.

**Impact** – C’est le premier médicament oral à réduire autant le cholestérol LDL, ce qui le rend plus facile à prendre pour beaucoup de personnes. Comme les injections peuvent être coûteuses ou compliquées, une pilule efficace pourrait aider plus de patients à atteindre des taux de cholestérol plus sûrs et à réduire leur risque de maladie cardiaque.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs mènent un autre essai pour vérifier si enlicitide diminue réellement le nombre de crises cardiaques et d’AVC. S’il est approuvé par les autorités sanitaires, il pourrait devenir un nouveau traitement plus simple.

**Phrase clé** – La nouvelle pilule enlicitide offre une option puissante et facile à prendre pour réduire le mauvais cholestérol de 60 %, aidant potentiellement des millions de personnes à prévenir les problèmes cardiaques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva píldora reduce colesterol malo en un 60 %, igualando inyecciones potentes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas tienen niveles altos de colesterol LDL, conocido como colesterol "malo", que puede bloquear el flujo sanguíneo y causar ataques cardíacos o accidentes cerebrovasculares. Los tratamientos actuales incluyen pastillas llamadas estatinas y inyecciones, pero las inyecciones pueden ser difíciles de usar.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva píldora llamada enlicitide fue probada en un gran ensayo clínico con casi 3,000 participantes. Tomada una vez al día, redujo el colesterol LDL en alrededor del 60 %, la misma cantidad que los medicamentos inyectables fuertes. La mayoría ya tomaba estatinas pero aún tenía colesterol alto. El ensayo duró 24 semanas y los resultados se mantuvieron tras un año.

**Impacto** – Es el primer medicamento oral que reduce tanto el colesterol LDL, lo que facilita su uso para muchas personas. Como las inyecciones pueden ser caras o incómodas, una píldora eficaz podría ayudar a más pacientes a alcanzar niveles seguros de colesterol y reducir su riesgo de enfermedades del corazón.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están realizando otro ensayo para ver si enlicitide realmente reduce ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares. Si es aprobado por las autoridades, podría convertirse en una nueva opción de tratamiento más sencilla.

**Frase clave** – La nueva píldora enlicitide ofrece una opción potente y fácil de tomar para reducir el colesterol malo en un 60 %, ayudando potencialmente a millones a prevenir problemas del corazón.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized human heart surrounded by gently flowing golden threads representing LDL cholesterol being gently pulled away by a glowing, pill-shaped figure emitting soft light, set against a calm, natural background of muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Scientists turn probiotic bacteria into tumor-hunting cancer killers
articles.summary: Scientists have engineered probiotic bacteria to act as tumor-seeking drug factories. In mice, these bacteria infiltrated tumors and produced a cancer-fighting drug right where it was needed. This targeted approach could make treatments more effective and reduce side effects. More research is needed before it can be tested in people.
articles.content:
Cancer affects millions of people worldwide each year, and treating it remains difficult because of how complex the disease can be. New findings published March 17th in the open-access journal PLOS Biology by Tianyu Jiang of Shandong University in Qingdao, China, and colleagues highlight a possible new strategy. The researchers show that Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN) can be modified to carry anticancer compounds and target tumors in mice.

Bacteria naturally live in the human body and influence both health and disease. Scientists are exploring whether these microbes can be redesigned to fight cancer, although their effectiveness as treatments is still uncertain.

To test this idea, the team engineered the probiotic strain Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN) so it could produce Romidepsin (FK228), an FDA-approved drug with anticancer properties. Through genetic and genomic engineering, they developed a version of the bacteria capable of generating this drug. They then created a mouse model by introducing breast cancer tumor cells and treated the mice with the modified bacteria.

Tumor Colonization and Targeted Drug Release

The experiments showed that EcN was able to accumulate inside tumors and release Romidepsin FK228 in both laboratory and live animal settings under different conditions. This allowed the bacteria to function as a targeted treatment, delivering the drug directly to tumor sites.

Even so, more research is needed. The approach has not yet been tested in humans, and future studies will need to examine possible side effects as well as strategies for safely removing the bacteria after treatment. These factors could influence how useful engineered EcN becomes as a cancer therapy.

A Dual-Action Cancer Therapy Strategy

According to the authors, "The probiotic strain Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN), a potential member of tumor-targeting bacteria, shows great promise for cancer treatment. By leveraging engineered EcN, we can design a bacteria-assisted, tumor-targeted therapy for the biosynthesis and targeted delivery of small-molecule anticancer agents. Our mouse-model study establishes a solid foundation for engineering bacteria which are capable of producing small-molecule anticancer drugs and engaged in bacteria-assisted tumor-targeted therapy, paving the way for future advancements in this field."

The authors add, "Escherichia coli Nissle 1917's tumor colonization synergizes with Romidepsin's anticancer activity to form a dual-action cancer therapy."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where engineered probiotic bacteria are used to target tumors and deliver cancer-fighting drugs directly, potentially improving cancer treatment effectiveness and reducing side effects. The research has broad implications for cancer therapy, a major global health challenge, and provides detailed context about the study and its promising results in animal models.
articles.personality_title: Scientists engineer probiotic bacteria to deliver cancer drugs directly to tumors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer is a disease that affects many people, and treating it is often difficult because cancer cells can be hard to target without harming the rest of the body. Scientists are looking for new ways to make cancer treatments more effective and safer.

**What happened** – Researchers in China changed a type of helpful bacteria called Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN), which normally lives in the body, so that it can produce a cancer-fighting drug named Romidepsin (FK228). They tested this in mice with breast cancer tumors. The bacteria were able to travel into the tumors and release the drug right where it was needed.

**Impact** – This new method is special because it uses bacteria to find and attack tumors directly. This could make cancer treatments work better and cause fewer side effects, since the drug is delivered only to the tumor, not the whole body. The study showed this worked well in mice, which is an important step forward.

**What's next step** – Scientists need to do more tests to make sure this approach is safe and effective in humans. They also have to figure out how to safely remove the bacteria after treatment. If successful, this could lead to new kinds of cancer therapies using engineered bacteria.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created bacteria that can carry and release cancer drugs directly inside tumors in mice, opening the door to more targeted cancer treatments in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques transforment des bactéries probiotiques en transporteurs de médicaments anticancéreux ciblant les tumeurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer touche beaucoup de personnes, et le traiter est difficile car les cellules cancéreuses sont difficiles à cibler sans abîmer le reste du corps. Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles façons de rendre les traitements plus efficaces et plus sûrs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en Chine ont modifié une bactérie bénéfique appelée Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN), qui vit normalement dans le corps, pour qu’elle produise un médicament anticancéreux nommé Romidepsine (FK228). Ils ont testé cela sur des souris avec des tumeurs du sein. Les bactéries sont capables de pénétrer dans les tumeurs et de libérer le médicament directement là où il est nécessaire.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est spéciale car elle utilise des bactéries pour trouver et attaquer directement les tumeurs. Cela pourrait rendre les traitements du cancer plus efficaces et causer moins d’effets secondaires, car le médicament est délivré uniquement à la tumeur, pas à tout le corps. L’étude a montré que cela fonctionnait bien chez les souris, ce qui est une avancée importante.

**Étape suivante** – Les scientifiques doivent faire plus de tests pour s’assurer que cette approche est sûre et efficace chez l’humain. Ils doivent aussi trouver comment éliminer les bactéries après le traitement. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements du cancer utilisant des bactéries modifiées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé des bactéries capables de transporter et de libérer des médicaments anticancéreux directement dans les tumeurs chez la souris, ouvrant la voie à des traitements du cancer plus ciblés à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos modifican bacterias probióticas para llevar medicamentos anticancerígenos directamente a los tumores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer afecta a muchas personas y tratarlo es complicado porque las células cancerosas son difíciles de atacar sin dañar el resto del cuerpo. Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de hacer los tratamientos más efectivos y seguros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en China modificaron un tipo de bacteria beneficiosa llamada Escherichia coli Nissle 1917 (EcN), que normalmente vive en el cuerpo, para que produzca un medicamento contra el cáncer llamado Romidepsina (FK228). Probaron esto en ratones con tumores de mama. Las bacterias lograron entrar en los tumores y liberar el medicamento justo donde se necesitaba.

**Impacto** – Este método es especial porque usa bacterias para encontrar y atacar los tumores directamente. Esto podría hacer que los tratamientos contra el cáncer sean más efectivos y tengan menos efectos secundarios, porque el medicamento se entrega solo en el tumor, no en todo el cuerpo. El estudio mostró que funcionó bien en ratones, lo que es un paso importante.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos necesitan hacer más pruebas para asegurarse de que este método sea seguro y efectivo en humanos. También deben encontrar cómo eliminar las bacterias después del tratamiento. Si tiene éxito, podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos contra el cáncer usando bacterias modificadas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos crearon bacterias que pueden llevar y liberar medicamentos anticancerígenos directamente dentro de tumores en ratones, abriendo el camino a tratamientos contra el cáncer más dirigidos en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, friendly-looking cluster of blue-green probiotic bacteria shaped like Escherichia coli gently surrounding and illuminating a stylized, abstract tumor represented as a softly pulsing, semi-transparent pink sphere, with tiny golden droplets symbolizing anticancer drug release flowing from the bacteria into the tumor, all set against a simple, natural beige background.

articles.title: Stress can cause eczema to flare up – now we know why
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00876-3Scientists have identified the neurons that worsen the condition during stress.
articles.content:
Soothing the skin can help reduce eczema flare-ups. Credit: Ladanifer/iStock via Getty

For people with eczema, stress can trigger flare-ups and worsen their itchy rashes. But the link between stress and skin inflammation has been unclear.

Now, researchers have identified a network of neurons that respond to stress by activating immune cells in the skin, fuelling eczema symptoms1.

The findings, published in Science today, come from a mouse model of atopic dermatitis (AD) — a type of chronic eczema that affects more than 200 million people worldwide.

Read the paper: Maternal stress triggers early-life eczema through fetal mast cell programming

The study shows “how a feeling, such as psychological stress, can translate into a biological event, namely inflamed skin”, says co-author Shenbin Liu, a neurobiologist at Fudan University in Shanghai, China.

Allergic skin diseases, such as AD, are caused by overactive immune responses that attack the body’s own skin cells. Some people with the condition have a build-up of immune cells called eosinophils in the affected skin tissues, which exacerbates inflammation. But what drives these cells to the skin and activates them in AD hasn’t been clear.

Itchy cells

In an analysis of skin biopsies and blood samples from 51 people with AD, the researchers found that those who reported high stress levels had more severe skin inflammation and higher levels of eosinophils than did participants who reported low stress levels.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying the neural mechanism linking stress to eczema flare-ups, a chronic condition affecting over 200 million people worldwide. This breakthrough improves understanding of the disease's biological basis, potentially guiding better treatments and improving quality of life for many.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how stress worsens eczema symptoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Eczema is a skin condition that causes itchy, inflamed patches. Many people with eczema notice their symptoms get worse when they feel stressed, but until now, scientists didn’t know exactly why.

**What happened** – Researchers from Fudan University in China studied mice with a type of eczema called atopic dermatitis (AD), which affects over 200 million people worldwide. They discovered a group of nerve cells that respond to stress by activating immune cells in the skin. These immune cells, called eosinophils, cause more inflammation and make eczema symptoms worse. The team also looked at skin and blood samples from 51 people with AD and found that those feeling more stressed had more eosinophils and worse skin inflammation.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have clearly shown how feelings of stress can cause real physical changes in the skin that lead to eczema flare-ups. Understanding this connection helps explain why stress makes eczema worse and points to new ways doctors might treat or prevent flare-ups by targeting these nerve signals.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to develop treatments that calm these stress-sensitive nerves or block the immune cells they activate. This could lead to new medicines that reduce eczema symptoms, especially during stressful times.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found the nerve cells that link stress to eczema flare-ups, opening the door to better treatments for this common skin condition.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment le stress aggrave l’eczéma
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’eczéma est une maladie de la peau qui provoque des plaques rouges et qui démangent. Beaucoup de personnes atteintes remarquent que leurs symptômes s’aggravent quand elles sont stressées, mais jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques ne savaient pas pourquoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Fudan en Chine ont étudié des souris atteintes d’un type d’eczéma appelé dermatite atopique (DA), qui touche plus de 200 millions de personnes dans le monde. Ils ont découvert un groupe de cellules nerveuses qui réagissent au stress en activant des cellules du système immunitaire dans la peau. Ces cellules, appelées éosinophiles, provoquent plus d’inflammation et aggravent les symptômes de l’eczéma. L’équipe a aussi analysé des échantillons de peau et de sang de 51 personnes avec la DA et a constaté que celles qui étaient plus stressées avaient plus d’éosinophiles et plus d’inflammation.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques montrent clairement comment le stress peut provoquer des changements physiques dans la peau qui déclenchent une poussée d’eczéma. Comprendre ce lien aide à expliquer pourquoi le stress aggrave l’eczéma et ouvre la voie à de nouveaux traitements en ciblant ces signaux nerveux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent développer des traitements qui calment ces nerfs sensibles au stress ou bloquent les cellules immunitaires qu’ils activent. Cela pourrait aboutir à de nouveaux médicaments pour réduire les symptômes de l’eczéma, surtout en période de stress.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont identifié les cellules nerveuses qui relient le stress aux poussées d’eczéma, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs traitements pour cette maladie de peau courante.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo el estrés empeora los síntomas del eczema
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El eczema es una enfermedad de la piel que causa parches rojos y picazón. Muchas personas con eczema notan que sus síntomas empeoran cuando están estresadas, pero hasta ahora, los científicos no sabían por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Fudan en China estudiaron ratones con un tipo de eczema llamado dermatitis atópica (DA), que afecta a más de 200 millones de personas en todo el mundo. Descubrieron un grupo de células nerviosas que responden al estrés activando células del sistema inmunológico en la piel. Estas células, llamadas eosinófilos, causan más inflamación y empeoran los síntomas del eczema. También analizaron muestras de piel y sangre de 51 personas con DA y encontraron que quienes estaban más estresados tenían más eosinófilos y más inflamación en la piel.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que los científicos muestran claramente cómo el estrés puede causar cambios físicos en la piel que provocan brotes de eczema. Entender esta conexión ayuda a explicar por qué el estrés empeora el eczema y apunta a nuevas formas en que los médicos podrían tratar o prevenir estos brotes enfocándose en estas señales nerviosas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan desarrollar tratamientos que calmen estos nervios sensibles al estrés o bloqueen las células inmunes que activan. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos para reducir los síntomas del eczema, especialmente en momentos de estrés.

**Una frase clave** – Los científicos han encontrado las células nerviosas que conectan el estrés con los brotes de eczema, abriendo la puerta a mejores tratamientos para esta condición común de la piel.
